title: Sarah Farnsworth Hatch
text:
Hello! I’m Sarah, and I am a developmental psychologist and fourth-year PhD student at Harvard Graduate School of Education.
I draw on implementation and psychology frameworks to explore how to better support children’s positive social and emotional development. My research consists of a) evaluating caregiving practices and interventions and b) examining the beliefs and behaviors of the adults who are expected to be on board with such practices. Although I do conduct work internationally, I’m currently focusing on the developmental views of parents across the U.S. political spectrum.
Before my doctoral program, I worked as an education specialist at the University of Pennsylvania’s Implementation Science Center. I received a MSEd from the University of Pennsylvania’s Graduate School of Education and a BS in Sociology from Brigham Young University.
Check out my projects tab to see what I’m currently working on or visit my Google Scholar for my most recent academic publications.

title: Ongoing Projects
text:
I am a primary investigator on several projects examining what caregivers believe about social and emotional learning (SEL) and other politicized topics, and how those beliefs shape subsequent educational preferences. My work is funded by the Interdisciplinary Mind Grant from the Mind, Brain, Behavior Initiative at Harvard. I am also an active contributor in Stephanie Jones’ EASEL lab and Dana McCoy’s SEED lab.

Independently-Led Research

A Closer Look at Political Conservative’s Views of SEL: I conducted relational interviews in the Salt Lake and Utah Valleys to examine politically conservative parents’ views of SEL (manuscript under review).
Features of SEL that Drive Opposition: I used conjoint survey methods to explore differences in Republican and Democrat parents’ (N=705) SEL-related preferences (contact for preliminary findings; analysis ongoing).
Political and Psychological Predictors of Parents’ School Censorship: I used an experimental paradigm to examine predictors of parents’ book censorship behaviors, using a mix of political and social psychology measures. In partnership with Hannah Castner. (manuscript under review).
Parenting Self-Efficacy, Psychological Safeguarding, and Censorship: Drawing on Developmental Niche Theory and Item Response Theory, I developed and tested the validity evidence of two new measures of parenting beliefs which may predict parents’ censorship behaviors (contact for preliminary findings; analysis ongoing).



Collaborative Research

Describing and Predicting Implementation Fidelity I used Growth Curve Models to examine patterns of SEL implementation in after-school settings in Nebraska (manuscript in preparation, advised by Stephanie Jones).
Impacts of After-school SEL in Nebraska: I provided logistical support and supported data management and collection for a large, state-wide RCT of SEL Kernels (manuscript in preparation, led by Stephanie Jones).
Impacts of Mobile Data Credits on Caregivers’ Use of a Digital Parenting Intervention: We find that small mobile data credits support extended use of an efficacious parenting intervention in high altitude Andean Peru (manuscript in preparation, advised by Lena Jaggi).

title: Ongoing Projects
text:
With Stephanie Jones (EASEL Lab)

Analyzing implementation data and impacts from an RCT on SEL in after-school settings in Nebraska.
Analyzing implementation data from the national rollout of a trauma-informed, locally adapted SEL curriculum in Ukraine.

title: Ongoing Projects
text:
With Dana McCoy (SEED Lab)

Analyzing implementation of an AI-based parenting app in Peru, which showed positive effects on children’s development.
Examining mechanisms connecting the app to these positive outcomes.
Investigating associations between specific parenting behaviors and children’s behavioral outcomes in this context.

title: Ongoing Projects
section: Parents’ Politicized Views of Education in the US
text:
Parents’ Politicized Views of Education in the US

Qualitative study examining conservative parents’ views of SEL (manuscript in preparation).
Conjoint survey on Democrat and Republican SEL preferences.

title: Ongoing Projects
text:
School Censorship Studies

Predicting school censorship behaviors using a mix of classic and novel political science and social psychology measures.
Novel scales looking at parents’ beliefs surrounding “psychological fragility” and “conversational self-efficacy” using advanced psychometric approaches.

title: Ongoing Projects
section: Parents’ Politicized Educational Views and Behaviors
text:
Parents’ Politicized Educational Views and Behaviors

Conservative views of SEL: I utilize relational interviews in my home community to examine variation behind parents’ views of SEL.
Predicting school censorship: I use an experimental paradigm and original data to examine predictors of parents’ school censorship behaviors, using a mix of classic and novel political science and social psychology measures.
Parenting beliefs and censorship: using Item Response Theory, I develop and test the validity evidence of two new measures of parenting beliefs which may predict parents’ censorship behaviors.
SEL opposition experiment: I use conjoint survey methods to explore differences in Republican and Democrat parents’ SEL-related preferences.
